This week at NASA:

GOES-T goes up, Webb mirror alignment continues successfully, and the first work platform moves away from our Artemis I Moon rocket.

Add more space to your life by visiting https://t.co/JSDausMuVv. https://t.co/bbvVMxKWwX

@zacallier One is much closer! They only appear to overlap because of a chance alignment. This alignment gives astronomers an opportunity: by measuring how dust in the foreground galaxy affects starlight from the background galaxy, they can map dust in the foreground galaxy’s spiral arms.

@leoalbuq1 Great question! Most lunar samples brought back to Earth by Apollo astronauts have been opened and studied. But some samples collected on the Apollo 15, 16 & 17 missions were kept unopened and pristine, so they could be analyzed with more advanced technologies available today.

Good things come to those who wait.

Teams will begin tapping one of the last unopened Moon rock samples acquired during Apollo 17, around 50 years ago. The goal is to learn more about the lunar surface in anticipation of upcoming @NASAArtemis missions: https://t.co/q6P2KpSSvp https://t.co/hRN5FRnC4j

@iishmalkhan Yes, our future mission Dragonfly will send an eight-bladed rotorcraft that flies like a drone to Titan. Dragonfly will study this ocean world's atmospheric and surface properties, subsurface ocean and liquid reservoirs, and complex carbon-rich chemistry: https://t.co/FnIocM0jcF

Twin galaxies? Look again 👀

From the perspective of our orbiting @NASAHubble telescope, these two galaxies in the Virgo constellation appear side-by-side... but they're actually more than 150 million light-years apart: https://t.co/0VfGwYiHvF https://t.co/OMoDwbjaaa

Assembly of our @EuropaClipper spacecraft is now underway at @NASAJPL.

Scheduled to launch in 2024, this @NASAAstrobio mission will conduct close flybys of Jupiter's icy moon Europa, to determine whether it has conditions suitable for life: https://t.co/oaQaOobdMV https://t.co/bR77H0HYNq

@iishmalkhan Our #Crew4 astronauts will be conducting research in our orbiting laboratory to help us learn how to live in space while making life better back on Earth. Stay tuned for more @Commercial_Crew updates as we get closer to launch!

We're getting ready for our next @Commercial_Crew mission to the @Space_Station with @SpaceX's Crew Dragon spacecraft. #Crew4 is scheduled to lift off from @NASAKennedy no earlier than April 15.

Here's what’s up in March!

Mars, Saturn, and Venus are catching up mid-month, our closest open star cluster makes an appearance, and suns in other solar systems get time to shine.

#ReadyToGOES? You know it. @NOAA's GOES-T separated from its @ULALaunch Centaur upper stage after a successful launch yesterday, March 1. The satellite is on its way to helping provide more accurate weather forecasts and data about our changing planet. https://t.co/pR6xXwzDVc https://t.co/9umLM3dfxn

A new satellite is on its way to geostationary orbit, where it will help meteorologists better predict local weather & issue life-saving forecasts as storms develop.

@RaffaeleDiPalma Yes, the @Space_Station will change its attitude by about 90 degrees before executing a Cygnus reboost.

@bfcultras Planets (typically) don’t fall into their star due to conservation of angular momentum. Their orbital motion around a star balances gravitational tugs. But some planets have unstable orbits, and do eventually fall into their stars! Note: The TRAPPIST-1 planets have stable orbits.

@piratefalcon325 @NASAWebb @NASAAstrobio Red dwarfs are truly horrible when they're young, bathing any potential planets in harmful radiation that could influence atmospheric chemistry and possibly strip off these fledgling planets' atmospheres. We're trying to better understand the possible effects of older red dwarfs.

@AshishR81366662 We know 3 of them are in the habitable zone — the right orbital distance to be warm enough for liquid water to exist on their surfaces. Astronomers will use @NASAWebb to look for signs of an atmosphere, the next step to finding out if life could survive: https://t.co/THNX8GjW3f

@UDAYKIR94731329 Artemis I will be the 1st integrated test of our @NASA_Orion crew module, @NASA_SLS rocket, and @NASAKennedy's ground systems. It'll send Orion on a journey around the Moon & assure its safe entry, descent, splashdown, and recovery on its return to Earth: https://t.co/f35LOCFLF7

@BJones79 Don't worry! DART can't change the orbit in a way that would send the asteroid closer to Earth. All it will do is slightly change the speed of the smaller asteroid, which will stay in orbit around the larger asteroid, as our expert explained here: https://t.co/WfV4SKLI7A

@Crosley_AZ Great question. The fresh food sent to the @Space_Station aboard Cygnus included apples, avocado, tomato, mango, baby carrots, grapefruit, pears, and blueberries. Also aboard: assorted cheeses and ice pops for a frozen treat!

@migzcuad @NASAWebb Light can travel faster than we have the technology to. That said, @NASAWebb will be focusing on studying the atmospheres of these planets to determine which molecules and elements (maybe the building blocks of life!) exist there and what they indicate about the world. (2/2)

@migzcuad @NASAWebb It depends on how far away those planets are! For example, the Trappist system is only 39.4 light years away, so we would be seeing the light that left it 39+ years ago. That doesn't mean we could travel there easily or quickly. (1/2)

@JLCDhome2018 @NASAWebb What is special about this orbit is that it lets the telescope stay in line with the Earth as it moves around the Sun. This allows the satellite's large sunshield to protect the telescope from the light and heat of the Sun and Earth (and Moon): https://t.co/rww0hBX9Ej

@scottparnapy @NASAWebb The sunshield will allow the telescope to cool down to a temperature below 50 Kelvin (-370°F, or -223°C) by passively radiating its heat into space. @NASAWebb’s MIRI instrument uses a cryocooler to reach its significantly lower operating temperatures of -447°F (7 kelvins).
@JPMajor @NASAWebb Good things come to those who wait! In the meantime, just know we've got a lot of exciting science up ahead: https://t.co/8zPS6MetB4 #UnfoldTheUniverse

@_Crazycrit @NASAWebb As Webb is orbiting L2, it's also moving around the Sun. Since Webb can only point to roughly half the sky at any given moment because it can’t look in the direction of Earth & the Sun, this affords Webb access to the entire sky over the course of a year: https://t.co/Fv0KXtzKLS

@ianwalkerxx @NASAWebb After @NASAWebb reaches Lagrange point 2 in a few weeks, it will take ~5 months to cool down, align its mirrors & calibrate its instruments before the science begins: https://t.co/2RH2EXQX7a #UnfoldTheUniverse

@bricetune They have a much bigger cryogenic vacuum chamber at @NASA_Johnson that was used on the Apollo program and we tested our mirror and instruments there. The spacecraft (sunshield and bus) were built at Northrop Grumman and they were connected to the mirror/instruments there. (2/2)
@bricetune As big as the vacuum chamber at Goddard is, it isn't big enough for all of Webb! We did test our instrument module there, but after that we had to move the hardware to NASA Johnson. (1/2)

@MetaOracle @NASAWebb Webb can #UnfoldTheUniverse through the infrared spectrum and also has the ability to see a little bit into visible light. Since infrared and visible light are next to each other on the spectrum, @NASAWebb's vision extends just a little bit into the red portion of the visible.
LIVE: @NASAWebb has completed the final stage of spacecraft deployment to #UnfoldTheUniverse. Mission experts discuss today's major milestone.  https://t.co/u2c50P4G0Y

@skybits We do expect tiny micrometeorite impacts, and the telescope was designed and tested for this.The sunshield has special seams to limit micrometeorid damage. Testing showed that micrometeoroids have negligible effects on the telescope's beryllium mirrors: https://t.co/Jx1ThKYsPL

Congratulations, @NASAWebb! You are fully deployed! 🥳

@Jeetpriyodey1 The Universe is now about 13.8 billion years old. We'll see a period of the Universe's history we haven't seen before, which is about 200 million years after the Big Bang, when the first galaxies started forming. https://t.co/vkI0lNdZxx

@jamesdmoore614 @NASAHubble @NASAWebb Webb will observe the Ultra Deep Field in our 1st year of science operations. It'll take Webb less than a day to see deeper into the universe than Hubble saw in 2 weeks of staring! It will find tens of thousand of galaxies that are too red & too faint for Hubble to detect.
@PatBarr52364730 The telescope has four science instruments which cover near-infrared through mid-infrared wavelengths, from 0.6 microns up through 28 microns: https://t.co/3dK4AwKDiA
@WangnickS The sunshield has special seams to limit meteorite damage. Testing showed that micrometeoroids have negligible effects on the telescope's beryllium mirrors: https://t.co/Jx1ThKYsPL

@WangnickS Debris isn't an issue a million miles away where we will orbit L2, and space is big so impacts from larger objects (asteroids, meteors) won't be an issue. We do expect tiny micrometeorite impacts, and the telescope was designed and tested for this.

@CougerMom @NASAWebb’s mirrors are made of beryllium because it's stable across a a wide range of temperatures. They’re then coated in a thin layer of gold to improve the mirror's reflection of infrared light. Here's more info: https://t.co/6eUrI3tOFQ

@PatBarr52364730 The Near Infrared Camera (NIRCam), @NASAWebb's primary imager, will cover the infrared wavelength range 0.6 to 5 microns. It will detect light from the earliest stars and galaxies! Details here: https://t.co/iQ0D48wAHH

@nachtsmeer The layers, each as thin as a human hair, are made of Kapton, a tough, high-performance plastic. Every layer is coated with aluminum, & the sun-facing side of the two hottest layers also have a doped-silicon (or treated silicon) coating to reflect the sun's heat back into space.

Why is @NASAWebb's sunshield shaped like a kite? In short, it's exactly the shape & size it needs to be.
 
The iconic profile lets the telescope tilt back and forth to observe more of the universe. Plus, it helps radiate heat from the sides. Take a look: https://t.co/YhpDZdWLb8 https://t.co/TjCXoFivq9

@JacksonBTT Splitting the sunshield into 5 layers allows heat to radiate out from between them, causing each successive layer to be cooler than the one below. The vacuum of space between each layer also acts as a great insulator. Learn more about the sunshield design: https://t.co/j1nRsAwHOL
